question_text: Which one of the following predicate formulae is NOT logically valid? Note that is a predicate formula without any free occurrence of x.

options.A: $\exists x ( p ( x ) \wedge W ) \equiv \exists x p ( x ) \wedge W$
options.C: $\exists x ( p ( x ) \to W ) \equiv \forall x p ( x ) \to W$
options.B: $\forall x ( p ( x ) W ) \equiv \forall x p ( x ) W$
options.D: $\forall x ( p ( x ) \lor W ) \equiv \forall x p ( x ) \lor W$

solution:
To determine which of the given predicate formulae is NOT logically valid, we need to analyze each option step by step.

**Option (A):** $\exists x ( p ( x ) \wedge W ) \equiv \exists x p ( x ) \wedge W$

This formula states that the existential quantifier applied to the conjunction of $p(x)$ and $W$ is equivalent to the conjunction of the existential quantifier applied to $p(x)$ and $W$. This is not generally true. The left-hand side means there exists an $x$ such that both $p(x)$ and $W$ are true. The right-hand side means that there exists an $x$ such that $p(x)$ is true, and $W$ is true. These two statements are not equivalent because the left-hand side requires that $W$ is true for some $x$, while the right-hand side requires that $W$ is true and there exists an $x$ such that $p(x)$ is true. Therefore, option (A) is not logically valid.

**Option (B):** $\forall x ( p ( x ) W ) \equiv \forall x p ( x ) W$

This formula states that the universal quantifier applied to the conjunction of $p(x)$ and $W$ is equivalent to the conjunction of the universal quantifier applied to $p(x)$ and $W$. This is not generally true. The left-hand side means that for all $x$, both $p(x)$ and $W$ are true. The right-hand side means that for all $x$, $p(x)$ is true, and $W$ is true. These two statements are not equivalent because the left-hand side requires that $W$ is true for all $x$, while the right-hand side requires that $W$ is true and for all $x$, $p(x)$ is true. Therefore, option (B) is not logically valid.

**Option (C):** $\exists x ( p ( x ) \to W ) \equiv \forall x p ( x ) \to W$

This formula states that the existential quantifier applied to the implication of $p(x)$ and $W$ is equivalent to the implication of the universal quantifier applied to $p(x)$ and $W$. This is not generally true. The left-hand side means that there exists an $x$ such that if $p(x)$ is true, then $W$ is true. The right-hand side means that if for all $x$, $p(x)$ is true, then $W$ is true. These two statements are not equivalent because the left-hand side only requires that there exists an $x$ for which the implication holds, while the right-hand side requires that the implication holds for all $x$. Therefore, option (C) is not logically valid.

**Option (D):** $\forall x ( p ( x ) \lor W ) \equiv \forall x p ( x ) \lor W$

This formula states that the universal quantifier applied to the disjunction of $p(x)$ and $W$ is equivalent to the disjunction of the universal quantifier applied to $p(x)$ and $W$.
